en.extract:
The banded yellow robin or olive-yellow robin (Eopsaltria placens) is a species of bird in the Australasian robin family Petroicidae that is found in New Guinea. Its natural habitats are subtropical or tropical moist lowland forest and subtropical or tropical moist montane forest.
It is threatened by habitat loss.


Taxonomy
The banded yellow robin was described in 1879 as Eopsaltria placens by the Australian zoologist, Edward Pierson Ramsay from a specimen collected in southeastern New Guinea. The specific epithet is Latin meaning "charming" or "pleasing". The species was subsequently placed in the genus Poecilodryas. Based on a 2011 molecular genetic study by Les Christidis and coworkers, it was moved into the resurrected genus Gennaeodryas, and is now placed in a more broadly defined Eopsaltria. The species is monotypic: no subspecies are recognised.


References


Further reading
del Hoyo, J.; Elliot, A. & Christie D. (editors). (2007). Handbook of the Birds of the World. Volume 12: Picathartes to Tits and Chickadees. Lynx Edicions. ISBN 978-84-96553-42-2

The '''banded yellow robin''' or '''olive-yellow robin''' ('''''Eopsaltria placens''''') is a species of [[bird]] in the Australasian robin family [[Petroicidae]] that is found in [[New Guinea]]. Its natural [[habitat]]s are [[subtropical or tropical moist lowland forest]] and subtropical or tropical moist [[montane forest]].
It is threatened by [[habitat loss]].

==Taxonomy==
The banded yellow robin was described in 1879 as ''Eopsaltria placens'' by the Australian zoologist, [[Edward Pierson Ramsay]] from a specimen collected in southeastern [[New Guinea]].<ref>{{ cite journal | last=Ramsay | first=E.P. | author-link=Edward Pierson Ramsay | year=1879 | title=Contributions to the zoology of New Guinea. Parts I and II | journal=Proceedings of the Linnean Society of New South Wales | volume=3 | pages=241–305 [272] | url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/6456651 | doi=10.5962/bhl.part.22241 }}</ref> The specific epithet is [[Latin]] meaning "charming" or "pleasing".<ref>{{ cite web | last=Jobling | first=James A. | title=placens | work=The Key to Scientific Names | url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/key-to-scientific-names/search?q=placens | publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology | access-date=13 November 2025 }}</ref> The species was subsequently placed in the genus ''[[Poecilodryas]]''.<ref>{{ cite book | editor1-last=Mayr | editor1-first=Ernst | editor1-link=Ernst Mayr | editor2-last=Cottrell | editor2-first=G. William | year=1986 | title=Check-list of Birds of the World | volume=11 | publisher=Museum of Comparative Zoology | place=Cambridge, Massachusetts | page=576 | url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/14484277 }}</ref> Based on a 2011 molecular genetic study by [[Leslie Christidis|Les Christidis]] and coworkers,<ref name=christidis>{{ cite journal | last1=Christidis | first1=L. | author1-link=Leslie Christidis | last2=Irestedt | first2=M. | last3=Rowe | first3=D. | last4=Boles | first4=W.E. | last5=Norman | first5=J.A. | year=2011 | title=Mitochondrial and nuclear DNA phylogenies reveal a complex evolutionary history in the Australasian robins (Passeriformes: Petroicidae) | journal=Molecular Phylogenetics and Evolution | volume=61 | issue=3 | pages=726–738 | doi=10.1016/j.ympev.2011.08.014 }}</ref> it was moved into the resurrected genus ''Gennaeodryas'',<ref>{{ cite book | editor1-last=Dickinson | editor1-first=E.C. | editor1-link=Edward C. Dickinson | editor2-last=Christidis | editor2-first=L. | editor2-link=Leslie Christidis | year=2014 | title=The Howard & Moore Complete Checklist of the Birds of the World | volume=2: Passerines | edition=4th | location=Eastbourne, UK | publisher=Aves Press | isbn=978-0-9568611-2-2 | page=260 }}</ref> and is now placed in a more broadly defined ''[[Eopsaltria]]''. The species is [[monotypic]]: no [[subspecies]] are recognised.<ref name=avilist>{{ cite web | author=AviList Core Team | date=2025 | title=AviList: The Global Avian Checklist, v2025 | doi=10.2173/avilist.v2025 | doi-access=free | url=http://www.avilist.org/checklist/v2025/ | access-date=13 November 2025 }}</ref>
